digest.intro: AI splinters its own coalition, math bends, and a country loses its land.

items.title: A Hacker Erased Romania's Entire Land Registry
items.blurb: After a failed extortion attempt, a single attacker entered Romania's cadastre agency using valid credentials, mapped the systems, then wiped both the databases and all backups — leaving the country's entire real-estate market legally paralyzed for over a week. Notaries can't record transactions; citizens can't prove they own their homes. The incident reframes the threat model for civil infrastructure: it's not about theft anymore, it's about erasure of the evidentiary layer that property law runs on.

items.title: China's Free AI Models Are Splitting the White House
items.blurb: Moonshot's Kimi K3 — free, open-weights, and reportedly competitive with GPT-class models — has fractured Trump's AI advisors into open-source advocates and closed-model protectionists, with David Sacks calling Anthropic's output "lobotomized" and Pentagon officials trading insults with OpenAI publicly. The core problem is structural: every smart free Chinese model erodes the revenue case for US frontier labs, whose valuations are propping up a meaningful slice of current economic optimism. China isn't just competing on capability — it's competing on price point, and zero is a hard price to beat.

sections.heading: The AI inflection that's harder to spin

items.title: AI Is Now Generating Counterexamples to Unsolved Math Problems Faster Than Humans Can Follow
items.blurb: In May 2026, ChatGPT disproved Erdős' Unit Distance conjecture — a decades-old open problem in discrete geometry — by connecting it to a 1960s theorem in number theory. Within a week, a system co-founded by Yann LeCun's company had auto-formalized the entire proof in Lean, the machine-checkable proof language, and got it verified. The author of this post is a formal verification mathematician who spent nine years arguing humans couldn't be trusted with technical proofs; his measured alarm about what's happening right now is worth reading carefully.

items.title: Reverse-Engineering Anything at Home Is Now Basically Free
items.blurb: Willison's observation is deceptively simple: coding agents didn't make reverse-engineering possible — it was always possible — they just collapsed the ROI calculus entirely. The psychological weight of "is this worth it if the API breaks next month" dissolves when rewriting is nearly free. For hardware tinkerers and makers, this is a genuine phase shift: devices that were practically closed are now practically open.

items.title: Who Actually Benefits From Banning Chinese Models?
items.blurb: Ben Thompson cuts straight to the incentive hypocrisy: the same labs lobbying to ban Chinese open-weights models for "distillation" trained their own models on unlicensed data at scale. His deeper point is an Aggregation Theory inversion — zero marginal cost software once favored demand aggregators, but AI's compute costs bring old industrial economics back, and open-weights Chinese models are the wrench in that machinery. The national security framing is real but also conveniently useful for incumbents with a revenue problem.

sections.heading: Things that want to be built

items.title: Gaussian Splat of Grace Cathedral
items.blurb: A beautifully executed Gaussian splat capture of Grace Cathedral in San Francisco — one of the most spatially dramatic Gothic interiors in the US, with its soaring nave and labyrinthine side chapels now navigable in-browser. For anyone working in WebGL or 3D, this is a benchmark for what the format can actually feel like when the subject and execution are matched well.

items.title: Shinjuku Station Modeled in Three.js
items.blurb: Someone took official Japanese government indoor mapping data for Shinjuku — the world's busiest station, notorious for being a near-impossible labyrinth with 200 exits — and rendered it in Three.js as a navigable 3D space. The subject is perfect for this treatment: a building that is already experienced as a non-Euclidean puzzle, now made literal.

items.title: Jelly UI: Soft-Body Physics for Ordinary HTML Form Controls
items.blurb: A dependency-free Web Components library that gives standard form elements — buttons, inputs, toggles — real soft-body physics: they squish, jiggle, and settle. It ships with WCAG AA color tokens and dark mode support, so it's not a toy. The gap between what you expect an interface to feel like and what this one does is exactly where the delight lives.

items.title: Ex Situ: A Map That Draws Arcs Between Where Objects Were Stolen and Where They Sit Now
items.blurb: Ex Situ is an open-source spatial index of displaced cultural artifacts — it maps looted or removed objects from their origin points to the Western museums that currently hold them, visualized as arcs across a global map. It turns the repatriation debate into a cartographic argument: geography as moral ledger. The format makes the scale of displacement legible in a way that lists and legal briefs never quite do.

facts.fact: ChatGPT disproved Erdős' Unit Distance conjecture in May 2026 by connecting it to a 1960s number theory theorem — and the proof was auto-formalized in Lean and machine-verified within a week.
After a hacker wiped Romania's entire land registry, citizens cannot legally prove they own their homes — the country's real-estate market has been at a legal standstill for over a week.

China's Kimi K3 is free and open-weights, which means every time a competitive Chinese model releases, US frontier AI labs lose the pricing argument that justifies their multibillion-dollar valuations.
Shinjuku Station — the world's busiest — has around 200 exits, and someone has now rendered its full interior as a navigable 3D map in Three.js using official Japanese government spatial data.

art_picks.title: Tati's Playtime
art_picks.creator: Jacques Tati
art_picks.why: A film built like a system — Paris as a vast, mirrored machine where modernist grids accidentally generate human comedy. Every frame is a generative artwork about how designed environments shape behavior without anyone noticing; it'll make you want to build something spatial and absurd.

art_picks.title: Selected Ambient Works Volume II
art_picks.creator: Aphex Twin
art_picks.why: Less an album than an environment — pieces that feel like they're rendering a physical space, pre-existing somewhere you've never been. The production is obsessively narrow in palette yet generates enormous atmospheric depth, the kind of unreasonable craft in a single register that tends to hook you.

art_picks.title: The Passage of the Migratory Birds (installation / textile works)
art_picks.creator: Anni Albers
art_picks.why: Albers treated weaving as a programming language — each textile is a rule system that generates visual music, the grid as expressive surface. The Anni Albers retrospective catalogue and her writing on 'weaving as diagram' is the deep rabbit hole; the work sits exactly at the edge where physical structure becomes something that feels like code.

events.name: Whitney Biennial 2026
events.venue: Whitney Museum of American Art, 99 Gansevoort St, Manhattan
events.why: 56 artists and collectives navigating interspecies kinship, infrastructure, geopolitical entanglement, and technological affinity — curated by mood and texture rather than thesis, with standout works including Young Joon Kwak's glitter-and-mirror body-cast spiral and Kelly Akashi's ghost chimney in cast glass; last weeks to catch it.

events.name: David Byrne / Saul Steinberg — Influence and Affinity
events.why: A pairing of two artists who both treated cultural systems, symbols, and urban life as raw material — Byrne's visual art in dialogue with Steinberg's legendary graphic world-building; closes July 31, last days.

events.name: Gabriel Orozco: Public Nature (Public Art Fund)
events.venue: JCDecaux Bus Shelters across New York City
events.why: Orozco's first commissioned photographic series — 10 new site-specific images exploring urban fabric installed across NYC bus shelters, turning the city's most overlooked infrastructure into a distributed gallery of found encounters and ephemeral sculpture.

events.name: Spirit of the Beehive w/ Choir Boy & Phantasia
events.venue: The Ruins at Knockdown Center, 52-19 Flushing Ave, Maspeth, Queens
events.why: Spirit of the Beehive's collage-like, sample-splattered indie-psych in a restored factory with towering ceilings — the venue's industrial architecture and the band's sprawling, immersive sets are a rare physical-sonic pairing.

events.name: MoMA PS1 Warm Up 2026 — Opening Night: Carlos Souffront + Wackies Showcase
events.venue: MoMA PS1 Courtyard, 22-25 Jackson Ave, Long Island City, Queens
events.why: The series' 28th season opens with Carlos Souffront (The Bunker NY) alongside a rare Wackies Showcase pairing Bronx reggae label founder Lloyd 'Bullwackie' Barnes with Basic Channel's Mark Ernestus — diasporic club history meeting avant-garde electronics in PS1's 50th-anniversary year, performed on a stage designed by artist Somnath Bhatt.
